## Code Review

This pull request significantly expands the AR Disc Golf application into a fully featured PWA, introducing a custom course creator, local storage-persisted game sessions, and an interactive game screen with map overlays and 3D AR guidance. Key feedback highlights several critical issues: a potential call stack overflow when encoding large course data, active geolocation watch leaks during tracking retries, decoding failures due to unpadded base64 strings, accumulated stale game sessions in local storage, and leaked wake lock sentinels when re-acquiring screen locks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The [consumer version of Gemini Code Assist on GitHub](https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/review-repo-code) is being sunset. Starting **June 18, 2026**, new organization installations will be blocked, and all code review activity will officially cease on **July 17, 2026**.
> For more details on the timeline and next steps, please review the [Help Documentation](https://developers.google.com/gemini-code-assist/docs/deprecations/consumer-code-review).

<!-- brin-pr-finding -->
Superagent found 1 security concern(s).

**Workflow runs on pull_request without explicit token permissions** Adding `pull_request` to an unprivileged workflow without `permissions:` leaves the GITHUB_TOKEN at repository default, potentially write-all on older repos. Add `permissions: {}` at the workflow top level and grant only the job-level permissions each job needs.
AI prompt ```text Check if this security scanner issue is valid. If so, understand the root cause and fix it. If appropriate, update or add tests. Keep the change focused and preserve intended behavior. P1 Workflow runs on pull_request without explicit token permissions The .github/workflows/lint.yml workflow was modified to add a pull_request trigger. There is no top-level permissions block. The default GITHUB_TOKEN permissions depend on the repository and organization settings; on repos created before February 2023 the default can be write-all. Because this workflow now runs on fork PRs, an unnecessarily permissive token is available to any code submitted by an external contributor, which widens the blast radius if a tool or dependency is ever compromised. Add a top-level permissions: {} block to default the token to no permissions, then add explicit job-level permissions to each job. For example: permissions: {} jobs: build: permissions: contents: read ```

**15 issues found** across 41 files
Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues) ```text Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P3: Invalid share-code feedback is styled like a success message, which can mislead users into thinking the import worked. The same `.import-message` class is used for both success and error text but is hard-coded to green; consider separate success/error styles (or a neutral default). P1: AR guidance can point in the wrong direction because relative `deviceorientation` alpha values are treated as true compass headings. Gate the alpha fallback on `event.absolute` so only north-referenced readings update heading. P2: Imported share codes can pass non-finite or non-integer hole values, which can produce invalid scoring/output (for example `Infinity` par from `1e309`). Tightening `isHole` to require positive integers for `number` and `par` would keep malformed course data out. P2: Using `String.fromCharCode(...bytes)` with a large encoded course can exceed argument limits and fail for bigger share payloads. Building the binary string iteratively (or in chunks) avoids this size-dependent runtime error before `btoa`. P2: Shared course data can become stale after post-save edits because the visible share code is not invalidated when hole pars/structure or name change. Consider clearing `shareCode` (and optionally status text) on any course mutation so copied codes always match current editor state. P2: GPS failures are silently swallowed, so users can be stuck with a disabled "at my location" action and no reason shown. Surface a status message in the watch error callback (especially for permission denial) so fallback behavior is clear. P2: Current-game resume can point at a missing session when localStorage write fails, because `CURRENT_GAME_KEY` is written even if `saveGame` fails. Gating the pointer update on successful session persistence keeps resume state consistent. P3: Starting a new round always writes a fresh `discgolf.game.[id]` entry, but previous session keys are never removed here. Over time this can accumulate stale game payloads in localStorage and eventually hit quota; cleaning up the prior current session when creating a new game would prevent that buildup. P1: Malformed stored sessions can be treated as valid, which can break resume/completion flow and allow out-of-range hole access. Tightening `isValidSession` to enforce index bounds and `completedAt` shape prevents invalid localStorage data from propagating into game logic. P3: Row keys can collide when a course has duplicate hole numbers, which can cause incorrect row reuse/highlighting updates. A unique key that includes index avoids DOM diff instability for malformed or imported data. P2: The total +/- can be incorrect during live rounds because it compares strokes against full-course par, including unplayed holes. Computing par from only holes with recorded strokes keeps the displayed relative score consistent with the per-hole rows. P2: Accuracy ring rendering is tied to truthiness, so a valid `0` accuracy value suppresses the GeoJSON feature. Checking `accuracy !== null` keeps behavior numeric instead of truthy/falsy. P1: After an initial geolocation failure, later watch updates can recover GPS but the game still stays in the error state with no map initialization. The watchPosition success path updates coordinates only; it would be safer to bootstrap map/error state there when the first valid fix arrives. P3: Missing test coverage: `needsOrientationPermission()` returning `true` is never asserted. The iOS path is only exercised implicitly through `requestOrientationPermission()` tests, but the predicate itself is never directly verified with `requestPermission` present. Consider adding a test like `'reports permission needed on iOS'` that sets `requestPermission` to a mock function and asserts `toBe(true)`. P1: This workflow now runs on `pull_request`, but token permissions are left implicit. That means `GITHUB_TOKEN` inherits repository defaults, which may be broader than this job needs. Adding an explicit top-level `permissions: {}` and then granting minimal job-level scopes (for example `contents: read`) keeps PR runs least-privileged. ```
**Tip**: cubic can generate docs of your entire codebase and keep them up to date. Try it [here](https://docs.cubic.dev/wiki/ai-wiki?utm_source=github).

**Workflow runs on pull_request without explicit token permissions** Adding `pull_request` to an unprivileged workflow without `permissions:` leaves the GITHUB_TOKEN at repository default, potentially write-all on older repos. Add `permissions: {}` at the workflow top level and grant only the job-level permissions each job needs.
AI prompt ```text Check if this security scanner issue is valid. If so, understand the root cause and fix it. If appropriate, update or add tests. Keep the change focused and preserve intended behavior. P1 Workflow runs on pull_request without explicit token permissions The .github/workflows/lint.yml workflow was modified to add a pull_request trigger. There is no top-level permissions block. The default GITHUB_TOKEN permissions depend on the repository and organization settings; on repos created before February 2023 the default can be write-all. Because this workflow now runs on fork PRs, an unnecessarily permissive token is available to any code submitted by an external contributor, which widens the blast radius if a tool or dependency is ever compromised. Add a top-level permissions: {} block to default the token to no permissions, then add explicit job-level permissions to each job. For example: permissions: {} jobs: build: permissions: contents: read ```
